title: 第 16 節
en: At that time, the whole drama of the really ancient literature of the Brahmans had been acted. The old language had changed, the old religion, after passing through many phases, had been super seded by a new faith : for however sceptical or conscientious we may be before admitting or reject ing the claims of the Brahmans in favour of an enormous antiquity of their sacred literature, so much is certain and beyond the reach of reasonable doubt1, that Sandrocottus, who by Greek writers 1 In my ' History of Ancient Sanskrit Literature,' published in 1859 (p. 274), I had tried to lay down some general principles on which I thought the dates of Greek history might to a certain extent be reconciled with some of the traditional dates of the Northern and Southern Buddhists. The conclusions at which I then arrived were that Sandrocottus or TTandragupta became king in 315 B.C., that he reigned 24 years, and was succeeded by Bindusara in 291 B.C.; that Bindusara reigned (25 or) 28 years, and was succeeded by Asoka in (266 or) 263 B.C. ; and that Asoka was formally inaugurated in (262 or) 259 B.C., reigned 37 years, and died in (215 or) 212 B.C. The great Council took place in the I7th year of his reign, therefore either (245 or) 242 B.C.

In my attempt at arriving at some kind of rough chronology for the Buddhistic age, I was chiefly guided by a number of native traditions bearing on the distance between certain events and Buddha's death. Thus we find: — (i) That 162 years were sup posed to have passed between Buddha's death and /tandragupta's accession, 315+ 162 = 477, this giving us 477 B.C. as the probable date of that event. (2) We found that 218 years were supposed to have passed between Buddha's death and Asoka's inauguration, 259 + 218 = 477, this giving us 477 B.C. as the probable date of that event.

I therefore proposed that 477 B.C. should provisionally be ac cepted as the probable date of Buddha's death, instead of 543 B.C., THE ANCIENT LITERATURE OF INDIA. 135 is mentioned as a child when Alexander invaded India, who after Alexander's retreat was king at Palibothra, who was the contemporary of Seleucus Nicator, and several times visited by Megasthenes, was the same as the .fiTandragupta of Indian litera ture, who reigned at Pa^aliputra, the founder of and I tried to strengthen that position by some other evidence available at the time.

An important confirmation of that hypothesis has lately been added by two inscriptions discovered by General Cunningham, and published by Dr. Biihler in the ' Indian Antiquary.' Dr. Biihler seems to me to have shown conclusively in his two articles that the writer of these inscriptions could have been no other but Asoka. Asoka in these two edicts states that he has been for a long time, or for more than 33^ years, an updsaka or worshipper of Buddha, and that during one year or more he has been a member of the Samgha. Now if Asoka was consecrated in 259, and became an updsaka three or four years later, 255 B. c., these inscriptions would have been put up in 255 — 33^ = 221 B. c. According to the same inscriptions, 256 years had passed since the depai'ture of Buddha (here, too, I accept Dr. Biihler's interpretation, not because all its difficulties are removed, but because, in spite of all difficulties, the inscription cannot well be interpreted differently) 221 + 256 = 477, this giving us 477 B.C. as the probable date of Buddha's death.
This confirmation was entirely unexpected, and becomes there fore all the more important.

I may add one other confirmation. Mahinda, the son of Asoka, became an ascetic in the sixth year of his father's reign, i.e. in 253 B.C. At that time he was 20 years of age, and must therefore have been born in 273 B.C. Between his birth and Buddha's death 204 years are supposed to have passed, 273 + 204 = 477, this giving us once more 477 B.C. as the probable date of Buddha's death.

I learn that so high an authority as General Cunningham has arrived at the same conclusion with regard to the date of Buddha's death, and had published it before the appearance of my ' History of Sanskrit Literature,' in 1859 ; but I do not know whether his argu ments were the same as those on which I chiefly relied.

a new dynasty, and the grandfather of Asoka. This Asoka was the famous king who made himself the patron of Buddhism, under whom the great Buddhist Council was held in 245 or 242 B.C., and of whose time we have the first inscriptions, still extant on rocks, in different parts of India. These inscriptions are not in Sanskrit, but in a language which stands to Sanskrit in the same relation as Italian to Latin. The days therefore, when Sanskrit was the spoken language of the people, were over in the third century B.C.

Buddhism, again, the religion of Asoka, stands in the same relation to the ancient Brahmanism of the Veda as Italian to Latin, or as Protestantism to Roman Catholicism. Buddhism, in fact, is only intelligible as a development of, and a reaction against, Brahmanism. As against those, therefore, who consider the whole of Indian literature a modern forgery, or against ourselves, when unwilling to trust our own eves, we have at least these two facts, on which we can rely : that, in the third century B. c., the ancient Sanskrit language had dwindled down to a mere volgare or Prakrit, and that the ancient religion of the Veda had developed into Buddhism, and had been superseded by its own offspring, the state religion in the kingdom of Asoka, the grandson of TTandragupta.

The Veda proclaimed as revealed. One of the principal points on which Buddhism differed from Brahmanism, was the sacred and re vealed character ascribed to the Veda. This is a point of so much historical importance in the growth of the early theology of India, that we must THE ANCIENT LITERATURE OF INDIA. 137 examine it more carefully. The Buddhists, though on many points merely Brahmanists in disguise, denied the authority of the Veda, as a divine reve lation ; this being so, we may advance another step, and ascribe to the theory of a divine inspiration of the Veda a pre-Buddhistic origin and prevalence.

At what time the claim of being divinely revealed and therefore infallible, was first set up by the Brahmans in favour of the Veda, is difficult to determine. This claim, like other claims of the same kind, seems to have grown up gradually, till at last it was formulated into a theory of in spiration as artificial as any known to us from other religions.

The poets of the Veda speak in very different ways of their compositions. Sometimes they declare that they have made the hymns, and they compare their work, as poets, with that of the carpenter, the weaver, the maker of butter (ghrfta), the rower of a ship (X, 116, 9)1.

In other places, however, more exalted sentiments appear. The hymns are spoken of as shaped by the heart (I, 171, 2 ; II, 35, 2), and uttered by the mouth (VI, 32, i). The poet says that he found the hymn (X, 67, i) ; he declares himself power fully inspired after having drunk the Soma juice (VI, 47, 3), and he compares his poem to a shower of rain bursting from a cloud (VII, 94, i), or to a cloud impelled by the wind (I, 116, i).

After a time the thoughts that rose in the heart O and were uttered in hymns, were called God-given 1 A most useful collection of passages bearing on this point may be found in Dr. J. Huir's ' Sanskrit Texts,' vol. III.

(I, 37, 4), or divine (III, 18, 3). The gods were supposed to have roused and sharpened the mind of the poets (VI, 47, 10) ; they were called the friends and helpers of the poets (VII, 88, 4 ; VIII, 52, 4), and at last the gods themselves were called seers or poets (I, 31, i). If the petitions addressed to the gods in the hymns of the poets were fulfilled, these hymns were naturally believed to be endowed with miraculous powers, the thought arose of a real intercourse between gods and men (I, 179, 2 ; VII, 76, 4), and the ideas of inspiration and revelation thus grew up naturally, nay in evitably in the minds of the ancient Brahmans.

By the side of it, however, there also grew up, from the very first, the idea of doubt. If the prayers were not heard, if, as in the contest between VasishzAa and Vi.svamitra, the enemy who had called on other gods, prevailed, then a feeling of uncer tainty arose which, in some passages of the hymns, goes so far as to amount to a denial of the most popular of all gods, Indra1.

If, however, the claims to a divine origin of the Veda had amounted to no more than these poetic thoughts, they would hardly have roused any violent opposition. It is only when the divine and infallible character of the whole Veda had been asserted by the Brahmans, and when the Brahmanas also, in which these claims were formulated, had been represented as divinely inspired and infallible, that a protest, like that of the Buddhists, becomes historically intelli gible. This step was taken chiefly during the Sutra period. Although in the Brahmawas the divine 1 See this subject treated in Lecture VI.

THE ANCIENT LITERATURE OF INDIA. 139 authority of the Vedas is asserted as a fact, it is not yet, so far as I know, used as an instrument to silence all opposition ; and between these two positions the difference is very great. Though sruti, the later tech nical name for revelation, as opposed to smriti, tradi tion, occurs in the Brahmanas (Ait. Br. VII, 9), it is not yet employed there to crush all doubt or opposi tion. In the old Upanishads, in which the hymns and sacrifices of the Veda are looked upon as useless, and as superseded by the higher knowledge taught by the forest-sages, they are not yet attacked as mere impositions.

That opposition, however, sets in very decidedly in the Sutra period. In the Nirukta (I, 15) Yaska quotes the opinions of Kautsa, that the hymns of the Veda have no meaning at all. Even if Kautsa be not the name of a real person, but a nickname only, the unquestioning reverence for the Veda must have been on the wane before the days of Yaska and Pafiini1. Nor is it at all likely that Buddha was the first and only denier of the sacred authority of the Veda, and of all the claims which the Brahmans had founded on that authority. The history of heresy is difficult to trace in India, as elsewhere. The writings of B?^'haspati, one of the oldest heretics, constantly quoted in later controversial treatises, have not yet been recovered in India. Without committing myself to any opinion as to his age, I shall state here some of the opinions ascribed to Bn'haspati, to show that 1 Panini was acquainted with infidels and nihilists, as may be seen from IV, 4, 60. Lokayata, another name applied to unbe lievers, from which Laukayatika, is found in the Garai ukthadi, and IV, 2, 60. Barhaspatya occurs in the commentary only, V, i, 121.
140 LECTURE in.

even the mild Hindu can hit hard blows, and still more in order to make it clear that the stronghold of Brahmanism, namely the revealed character of the Vedas, was riot a mere theory, but a very important historical reality.

In the * Sarva - darsana - samgraha ' (translated by Professor Co well, Pandit, 1874, P- I^)2}> the firs^ philosophical system of which an account is given, is that of the /Tarvaka, who follows the tenets of Bn'haspati. The school to which they belonged is called the Lokayata, i. e. prevalent in the world. They hold that nothing exists but the four elements, a kind of protoplasm, from which, when changed by evolu tion into organic body, intelligence is produced, just as the inebriating power is developed from the mixing of certain ingredients. The self is only the body quali fied by intelligence, there being no evidence for a self without a body. The only means of knowledge is perception, the only object of man, enjoyment.
But if that were so, it is objected, why should men of proved wisdom offer the Agnihotra and other Vedic sacrifices 1 To this the following answer is returned : —

' Your objection cannot be accepted as any proof to the contrary, since the Agnihotra, etc. are only useful as means of livelihood, for the Veda is tainted by the three faults of untruth, self-contradiction and tau tology. Then again the impostors, who call them selves Vedic pandits, are mutually destructive, as the authority of the 6rnanakawc?a (Upanishads) is over thrown by those who maintain that of the Karmakan^a (Hymns and Brahman as), while those who maintain the authority of the (rnanakan^a reject that of the Lastly, the three Vedas themselves THE ANCIENT LITERATURE OF INDIA. HI are only the incoherent rhapsodies of knaves, and to this effect runs the popular saying : — • ' The Agnihotra, the three Vedas, the ascetic's three staves, and smearing oneself with ashes, Brihaspati says, these are but means of livelihood for those who have no manliness or sense.'
And again it has been said by Bnhaspati :—
' If a beast slain in the (ryotishtoma rite will itself go to heaven, "Why then does not the sacrificer forthwith offer his own father ?
If the A$raddha produces gratification to beings who are dead, Then there too, in the case of travellers when they start, it is needless to give provisions for the journey.
If beings in heaven are gratified by our offering the /S'raddha here.
Then why not give the food down below to those who are stand ing on the house-top ?

While life remains, let a man live happily, let him feed on ghee, even though he runs into debt, When once the body becomes ashes, how can it ever return again 1 He who departs from the body goes to another world, How is it that he comes not back again, restless for love of his kindred ?
zh: 當時，婆羅門真正古老文學的整齣戲劇早已落幕。古老的語言已然改變，古老的宗教在經歷了許多階段之後，也被一種新的信仰所取代：因為無論我們在承認或否定婆羅門對其聖典擁有極其古老歷史的主張時，是多麼地抱持懷疑或審慎的態度，有一點是確鑿無疑、且超越合理懷疑範圍的¹，那就是希臘作家筆下的桑德羅科圖斯（Sandrocottus）——¹ 在我於 1859 年出版的《古梵語文學史》（第 274 頁）中，我曾試圖提出一些通則，據此我認為希臘歷史的年代在某種程度上或許能與北傳及南傳佛教的某些傳統年代相互調和。我當時得出的結論是：桑德羅科圖斯，亦即旃陀羅笈多（Chandragupta），於西元前 315 年成為國王，在位 24 年，並於西元前 291 年由賓頭娑羅（Bindusara）繼位；賓頭娑羅在位（25 或）28 年，並於（西元前 266 或）263 年由阿育王（Asoka）繼位；而阿育王於（西元前 262 或）259 年正式登基，在位 37 年，並於（西元前 215 或）212 年駕崩。大結集發生於他在位的第 17 年，因此時間點為（西元前 245 或）242 年。

在我試圖為佛教時代建立某種粗略年表時，主要依據一系列本土傳統，這些傳統涉及某些事件與佛陀涅槃之間的時間距離。因此我們發現：—（i）據信從佛陀涅槃到旃陀羅笈多（/tandragupta）即位之間經過了一百六十二年，315 + 162 = 477，這使我們推斷該事件的可能年代為西元前四七七年。（2）我們發現據信從佛陀涅槃到阿育王（Asoka）加冕之間經過了二百一十八年，259 + 218 = 477，這同樣使我們推斷該事件的可能年代為西元前四七七年。
因此，我提議應暫時將西元前 477 年視為佛陀涅槃的可能年代，而非西元前 543 年。印度古代文獻中提及，亞歷山大入侵印度時尚為孩童、亞歷山大撤退後成為波里伯特拉國王、與塞琉古一世‧尼卡託同時代、並多次接待麥加斯梯尼的那位君主，正是印度文學中的旃陀羅笈多（Chandragupta），其統治於波吒釐子城（Pāṭaliputra），為該城奠基者。我當時亦嘗試以其他可得證據強化此論點。

該假說近日獲得一項重要佐證，源自坎寧安將軍發現、由比勒博士刊載於《印度古物誌》的兩則銘文。在我看來，比勒博士在其兩篇文章中已確鑿證明，這些銘文的書寫者非阿育王莫屬。阿育王在這兩道敕令中宣稱，他長期（或超過三十三年）以來一直是佛陀的優婆塞（upāsaka）或信徒，並在一年或更長時間內成為僧伽（Saṃgha）的一員。若阿育王於公元前259年受灌頂，並在三、四年後（即公元前255年）成為優婆塞，則這些銘文應刻於公元前255年減去三十三年，即公元前221年。根據同一批銘文，自佛陀涅槃（此處我亦接受比勒博士的詮釋，並非因其已解決所有疑難，而是儘管困難重重，該銘文實難另作解讀）以來已歷經二百五十六年：公元前221年加256年，得出公元前477年，此即佛陀入滅的可能年代。
這項證實完全出乎意料，因此變得更加重要。
我可以再補充一項佐證。阿育王之子摩哂陀（Mahinda）在其父在位第六年出家為苦行者，即西元前253年。當時他年屆二十，因此應出生於西元前273年。從他出生到佛陀入滅之間，一般認為相隔204年：273 + 204 = 477，這使我們再次得出西元前477年為佛陀入滅的可能年代。
我得知，像康寧漢將軍（General Cunningham）這樣權威的學者，對於佛陀涅槃的年代也得出了相同的結論，並且在我1859年出版的《梵語文學史》問世之前就已發表；但我並不清楚，他所依據的論證是否與我主要倚賴的論點相同。
一個新的王朝，而這位旃陀羅笈多正是阿育王的祖父。阿育王是那位著名的國王，他成為佛教的庇護者，在其統治下，西元前245年或242年召開了偉大的佛教結集，而我們所擁有的最早銘文，至今仍留存於印度不同地區的岩石上，正是出自他的時代。這些銘文並非以梵文書寫，而是使用一種與梵文的關係類似於義大利文與拉丁文的語言。因此，梵文作為人民口語的時代，在西元前三世紀便已終結。

佛教，即阿育王（Asoka）的宗教，與吠陀（Veda）時代的古老婆羅門教（Brahmanism）之間的關係，正如義大利語之於拉丁語，或新教（Protestantism）之於羅馬天主教（Roman Catholicism）。事實上，唯有將佛教理解為婆羅門教的發展與反動，才能夠真正認識它。因此，面對那些認為整個印度文學皆為近代偽造的人，或是當我們不願相信自己雙眼所見時，至少可以信賴以下兩個事實：在西元前三世紀，古老的梵語（Sanskrit）已退化為單純的俗語（volgare）或波羅克利語（Prakrit）；而吠陀的古老宗教已發展為佛教，並被其自身的產物所取代，成為旃陀羅笈多（Chandragupta）之孫——阿育王王國中的國教。
佛教與婆羅門教的主要分歧之一，便在於吠陀被賦予的神聖與天啟性質。此點在印度早期神學發展中具有極其重要的歷史意義，我們必須更仔細地加以審視。佛教徒雖在許多方面僅是偽裝的婆羅門教徒，卻否認吠陀作為神聖天啟的權威；正因如此，我們可以再進一步推論：吠陀神聖靈感說在佛教興起之前便已存在且廣為流傳。
婆羅門（Brahmans）何時首次為吠陀（Veda）提出「神聖啟示、因而無誤」的主張，實難斷定。此一主張，如同其他同類主張，似乎是逐漸發展而成，最終被系統化為一套靈感啟示理論，其人為造作之程度，不亞於我們從其他宗教所知者。
吠陀詩人以非常不同的方式談論自己的作品。有時他們宣稱自己創作了讚歌，並以詩人身分將自己的作品比作木匠、織工、製酥油者（ghrta）、划船者（X, 116, 9）¹。
然而，在其他段落中，則出現了更為崇高的情感。頌詩被描述為由心靈所塑造（I, 171, 2；II, 35, 2），並由口舌所宣說（VI, 32, 1）。詩人聲稱他發現了這首頌詩（X, 67, 1）；他宣稱自己在飲用蘇摩汁液後（VI, 47, 3）獲得了充分的靈感，並將自己的詩篇比擬為從雲層中迸發的驟雨（VII, 94, 1），或比擬為被風驅動的雲朵（I, 116, 1）。
經過一段時間，心中湧現並以讚歌表達的思想，被稱為神所賜予的。¹ 關於此點，J. 霍爾博士的《梵文文本》第三卷中，收錄了極為有用的相關段落集。

(I, 37, 4) 或神聖的 (III, 18, 3)。人們認為諸神喚醒並磨礪了詩人的心智 (VI, 47, 10)；他們被稱為詩人的朋友與助手 (VII, 88, 4；VIII, 52, 4)，最終諸神本身也被稱為先知或詩人 (I, 31, i)。倘若詩人頌歌中向諸神提出的祈求得以應驗，這些頌歌自然被認為具有神奇的力量；於是產生了人神之間真實交流的想法 (I, 179, 2；VII, 76, 4)，而靈感與啟示的概念便自然而然地——甚至可以說是無可避免地——在古代婆羅門的心中萌生。
然而，從一開始，懷疑的念頭也隨之而生。若祈禱未蒙垂聽，若如婆私吒（Vasiṣṭha）與眾友（Viśvāmitra）之爭——呼喚他神的敵人竟佔上風，那麼不確定之感便油然而生；在頌詩的某些段落中，這種感受甚至強烈到足以否定眾神中最受崇敬的因陀羅（Indra）¹。
然而，若吠陀神聖起源的主張僅止於此等詩意之思，恐難激起強烈反對。唯有當婆羅門宣稱整部吠陀具有神聖無誤之特質，且連載明此等主張的《梵書》亦被視為神啟無謬時，佛教徒這類抗議才在歷史脈絡中顯得意義可解。此一轉折主要發生於《經書》時期。雖在《梵書》中，神聖¹（此主題詳見第六講）——
吠陀的權威雖被宣稱為事實，但就我所知，尚未被用作壓制一切反對意見的工具；而這兩種立場之間的差異極為巨大。雖然 *śruti*（天啟）——作為後世專指「啟示」的術語，與 *smṛti*（傳承）相對——在梵書（Ait. Br. VII, 9）中已出現，但尚未被用來壓制一切懷疑或反對。在古老的奧義書中，儘管吠陀的讚歌與祭祀被視為無用，並被林中聖賢所傳授的更高知識所取代，但它們尚未被攻擊為純粹的欺騙。

然而，此種對立在《經書》時期（Sutra period）便已明確出現。在《尼祿多》（Nirukta，I, 15）中，耶斯迦（Yaska）引述了考茨薩（Kautsa）的觀點，認為《吠陀》的讚歌毫無意義可言。即便考茨薩並非真實人物的姓名，而僅是一個綽號，但對《吠陀》的盲目崇敬在耶斯迦與波你尼（Pāṇini）¹ 的時代之前必定已逐漸式微。此外，佛陀絕不可能是第一位、也是唯一一位否定《吠陀》神聖權威、以及婆羅門（Brahmans）基於該權威所建立的一切主張的人。異端思想的歷史在印度與其他地方一樣難以追溯。最古老的異端之一——祭主仙人（Bṛhaspati）的著作，雖在後世論戰文獻中屢被引用，至今卻仍未在印度被重新發現。在未對其年代作出任何定論的前提下，我將在此陳述一些歸於祭主仙人的觀點，以說明¹ 波你尼已知曉不信者與虛無主義者，參見《波你尼經》IV, 4, 60。Lokāyata（順世派）——不信者的另一稱呼，由此衍生出Laukāyatika——見於Gaṇa ukthādi與IV, 2, 60。Bārhaspatya僅見於注釋中，V, 1, 121。

因此，宗教學的首要任務，便是去發現這些宗教的歷史起源，並追溯其發展過程。然而，我們必須謹記，宗教的起源並非單一，而是多元的。正如語言科學（the science of language）教導我們，語言並非源自單一源頭，而是從多個中心獨立萌生，宗教亦復如此。我們所稱的雅利安語系（Aryan）、閃語族（Semitic）與圖蘭語族（Turanian）語言，各自擁有獨立的起源；同樣地，我們也必須承認，雅利安宗教、閃族宗教與圖蘭宗教，其根源亦各自獨立。這並非否認人類心靈的統一性，而是承認其表現形式的多元性。 在追溯宗教的歷史發展時，我們首先遭遇的，便是所謂的「自然宗教」（Nature Religion）。這並非指人類崇拜自然本身，而是崇拜自然中那些被視為無限者（the Infinite）之顯現的現象。在雅利安民族中，我們看到這種自然宗教最古老的文獻，即《吠陀》（Veda），尤其是《梨俱吠陀》（Rig-Veda）。這些頌詩中充滿了對自然力量的讚美——對天（Dyaus）、對地、對風暴、對黎明、對太陽。然而，這些讚美並非對物質對象的崇拜，而是對隱藏於其後、賦予其生命與活動的無限力量的敬畏。這種宗教形態，我們稱之為「單一神教」（henotheism）或「輪換主神教」（kathenotheism），意即每一次祈禱或獻祭時，所呼喚的神祇被視為至高無上，其他神祇暫時退居幕後。這並非多神教（polytheism），因為它並不承認多個平等且獨立的神祇；它也不是一神教（monotheism），因為它尚未將所有神性歸於唯一的存在。它是一種過渡階段，一種對無限者的直覺，透過個別的自然現象來表達。 然而，這種對自然的崇拜，若缺乏更高層次的引導，極易墮入所謂的「語言的疾病」（a disease of language）。當人們遺忘了詞語最初只是象徵，而將其視為實體時，神話便由此產生。例如，當「黎明」（Ushas）這個詞，原本只是描述一種自然現象，後來卻被視為一個獨立的女神，並賦予她人格與故事，神話便開始增生。因此，比較神話學（comparative mythology）的任務，便是追溯這些神話的起源，揭示它們如何從語言的誤解中誕生。而比較神學（comparative theology）則更進一步，它不僅研究神話，更研究宗教教義、儀式與制度，試圖從中提煉出人類對無限者之追尋的永恆真理。 因此，宗教學（the science of religion）必須同時運用比較神話學與比較神學的方法。它必須像語言科學一樣，透過比較不同宗教的現象，來發現其共同的規律與發展原則。唯有如此，我們才能理解宗教的本質，並在人類心靈的歷史中，為其找到恰當的位置。
即使是溫和的印度教徒也能發出重擊，而這更是為了清楚表明，婆羅門教的堡壘——亦即吠陀經的天啟性質——並非僅僅是一種理論，而是一個非常重要的歷史現實。

在《攝一切見解集》（*Sarva-darśana-saṃgraha*，柯威爾教授譯，1874年，第1頁）中，首先被論述的哲學體系是遮縛迦（Cārvāka）的學說，此派遵從毗訶缽提（Bṛhaspati）的教義。其所屬學派稱為「路伽耶陀」（Lokāyata），意即「世間流行」。他們主張唯有四大元素存在，此即某種原質（protoplasm）；當此原質經演化轉變為有機身體時，便產生智性（intelligence），正如某些成分混合後會產生醉人之力。自我（self）僅是具備智性之身體，並無證據顯示存在脫離身體的自我。知識的唯一來源是知覺（perception），人類的唯一目標則是享樂。
但若果真如此，反對者便會問：為何那些公認有智慧的人仍要獻上阿耆尼霍特羅（Agnihotra）及其他吠陀祭儀¹？對此，答覆如下：——

> 你的反對意見不能視為任何反面證據，因為阿耆尼霍特羅（Agnihotra）等儀式僅是謀生手段，吠陀（Veda）本身已沾染三種缺陷：虛妄、自相矛盾與同語反覆。再者，那些自稱吠陀學者（Vedic pandits）的騙子們彼此攻訐——主張《業品》（Karmakāṇḍa，讚歌與梵書）權威者推翻《智品》（Jñānakāṇḍa，奧義書）的權威，而主張《智品》權威者又否定《業品》的權威。最後，三部吠陀本身不過是無賴們雜亂無章的狂言，民間諺語亦如是說：—— 「阿耆尼霍特羅、三部吠陀、苦行者的三杖、以及塗抹灰燼，祭主仙人（Bṛhaspati）有言：這些不過是缺乏勇氣與智慧者的謀生之道。」
又，彼有言曰（Bṛhaspati 雲）：
> 若在（Jyotiṣṭoma 祭儀中）被宰殺的牲畜本身會昇天，「那麼為何獻祭者不立即獻上自己的父親呢？」
> 若對亡者行無信施（aśraddhā）能令其得滿足， > 則旅人啟程時，亦無需為其備辦道糧。
若天上眾生因我們在此處供奉*śrāddha*（祖先祭儀）而感到歡喜。
那麼，為何不將下方的食物，遞給那些站在屋頂上的人呢？
只要生命尚存，人就該快樂地活著，讓他享用酥油（ghee），即使負債累累也無妨。一旦身體化為灰燼，它又如何能再回來呢？¹ 離開身體的人前往另一個世界，為何他不再回來，因眷戀親人而焦躁不安？
